coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of biomass derived carbon dots via microwave-assisted method for selective detection of Fe3+ ions in an aqueous medium
coredata.dc:creator: George, Hema Sindhuja
Selvaraj, Hosimin
Ilangovan, Andivelu
Chandrasekaran, Karthikeyan
Kannan, Velu Rajesh
Parthipan, Punniyakotti
Almutairi, Bader O.
Balu, Ranjith
coredata.dc:description: Heavy metal ion represents the most serious issues to the environment and human beings. Detection and removal of heavy metal ions in the water bodies are essential. Generally, carbon dots are used as a good fluorescence sensor to sense heavy metal ions. In the present study, Papaya seeds are successfully modified into water-soluble carbon dots by one-step microwave-assisted method. Synthesised carbon dots average size was about 1.45 nm with spherical shape analysed by HRTEM and the presence of decorated functional groups was analysed by FTIR. Papaya seeds carbon dots (PPYCDs) emit bright blue colour under UV radiation. PPYCDs emits a fluorescence emission peak at 405 nm with an excitation wavelength of 335 nm. PPYCDs have the greatest quenching efficiency with Fe3+ ions and display a rapid and good sensitivity due to the decorated functional groups present on them. The Fe3+ ions detection limit was about 2.35 μMol and these ecofriendly carbon dots being synthesised by a simple procedure without any complicated instrumentation and toxic reagents, paves a way for the sensor applications effectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Emission
Detection
Quenching
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Ferric ion in aqueous medium

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of synthesis of Carbon dots.

A. UV–Visible spectra of PPYCDs (Blue and brown colour of CDs with UV light and normal light) b. Emission and excitation fluorescence of PPYCDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) Fluorescence emission spectra for PPYCDs with different excitation wavelength (b) Fluorescence emission wavelength of PPYCDs with a different excitation wavelength.
Photostability of carbon dots a) Irradiation of UV light at 365 nm b) Storage at room temperature.
FTIR spectra of papaya seeds powder and synthesised papaya seeds carbon dots (PPYCDs).
a and d) HR-TEM image of the PPYCDs b) Average particle size 1.45 nm c) interplanar spacing of PPYCDs.
A) Fluorescence emission spectra for PPYCDs with the addition of various metal ions (10 mmol). b) F/F0 of PPYCDs with various metal solutions.

A) Fluorescence spectra of PPYCDs with different concentrations of Fe3+ with excitation wavelength 335 nm b) Various Fe3+ ion concentration vs. Fluorescence intensity (inset linear detection range R2 value is 0.994).
Summarized the Biomass derived carbon dots and application of ferric ion detection.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of biomass derived carbon dots via microwave-assisted method for selective detection of Fe3+ ions in an aqueous medium

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heavy metal ion represents the most serious issues to the environment and human beings. Detection and removal of heavy metal ions in the water bodies are essential. Generally, carbon dots are used as a good fluorescence sensor to sense heavy metal ions. In the present study, Papaya seeds are successfully modified into water-soluble carbon dots by one-step microwave-assisted method. Synthesised carbon dots average size was about 1.45 nm with spherical shape analysed by HRTEM and the presence of decorated functional groups was analysed by FTIR. Papaya seeds carbon dots (PPYCDs) emit bright blue colour under UV radiation. PPYCDs emits a fluorescence emission peak at 405 nm with an excitation wavelength of 335 nm. PPYCDs have the greatest quenching efficiency with Fe3+ ions and display a rapid and good sensitivity due to the decorated functional groups present on them. The Fe3+ ions detection limit was about 2.35 μMol and these ecofriendly carbon dots being synthesised by a simple procedure without any complicated instrumentation and toxic reagents, paves a way for the sensor applications effectively.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanoparticles are the emerging materials used in various research fields and applications due to their smaller size. In recent years, researchers have focused in a new kind of carbon-based nanomaterials with fluorescent nature . Carbon dots – a zero-dimension carbon-based nanomaterial with a size of less than 10 nm gathers promising interest among researchers for its fascinating features such as bright fluorescence, biocompatibility and photostability etc.[1]. Most of the carbon dots are present in the form of amorphous nature and are decorated by different types of oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxyl, carboxylic, epoxy etc.[2]. The CDs have unique size, structure and physicochemical properties like high quantum yield, photostability, low toxicity, biocompatibility, water solubility, good conductivity etc. Based on these properties, carbon dots are used in various fields such as chemical sensing[3,4] bio imaging[5], photo catalysis[6] dye degradation [7] and energy storage applications[8]. An accidental discovery of carbon dots performed by Xu et. al. in 2004 during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes laid the foundation for the synthesis of carbon dots. Followingly Sun et al. in 2006 synthesised stable photoluminescent carbon dots with different sizes[19]. Carbon dots are mainly derived from biomass which means a biodegradable fraction of the products, biological origin like agriculture sources [10] seeds [11], fruits[12,13], flowers [6]leaves [14], biowaste[15] etc. Doping carbon dots (N, S) are also prepared using various biomass which may enhance the optical properties like emission, detection of metal ions etc [16,17]. Chionanthus retusus fruit was used to prepare the N-doped carbon dots which show the visible region emission spectra and the wavelength was about 425 nm [18]. These carbon dots can be used for various applications such as catalytic activity, bioimaging[13,19], metal detection[16,20], fluorescent ink[21] etc. Nowadays researchers are focused in technology to recover or develop useful materials/products from polluted wastes like biomass, domestic waste and industrial waste[22–30] which also helps to reduce environmental pollution and retain sustainability.

The nature of the carbon dots depends upon the source (i.e. natural precursor) and synthesis methods. The two different ways of carbon dots synthesis are top-down and Bottom up approaches. Chemical ablation, laser ablation, electrochemical synthesis and arc discharge methods are classified as Top-down processes where bulky molecules are converted into smaller-sized particles. Microwave, hydrothermal, pyrolysis and solvothermal methods are called bottom-up processes.[31]. Among these processes, microwave-assisted synthesis is one of the best ways to synthesise eco-friendly non-toxic carbon dots. Recently, Microwave synthesis has acquired more attention in the scientific community due to its very low reaction time (a few minutes), eco-friendly, energy-efficient, simple technique and uniform heating[32]. In microwave synthesis, the organic macromolecule compounds are involved in the carbonisation process in the presence of acidic or alkali environments for a very short period. Zhue et al. 2009 synthesised carbon dots with good physicochemical properties using the microwave-assisted process for the first time [33]. Since microwave-assisted synthesis produces multifaceted carbon dots, it sparks to go in for this easiest and most efficient approach.

Heavy metal ions such as Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Fe2+ and Fe3+ contamination create serious issues for humans, animals, plants and the environment[34]. If the concentration level increases or decreases, it affects/collapses the environmental system and causes various diseases to human health[35]. Ferric ion is the most abundant transition metal ion which plays a vital role in biological and environmental systems. Commonly Fe3+ ions are present in the human body in various places like the spleen, liver and bone marrow cells which also involve in the biological process of oxygen transport, metabolism of RNA etc. The enormous change in concentration of Fe3+ ion has greater impact in humans leading to a complicated diseases like anaemia, cancer etc. Prolonged exposure to these toxic metals in any way creates a threatening issue and causes a severe disorder in the life system. Hence, we are in need of the hour to reduce the pollution and in-depth to detect or remove the heavy metal ions from water sources. Since the Fe3+ ion usage cannot be minimized or avoided, its level in the discharge to the water resources could be monitored forcibly. Even though, various materials like nanoparticles, metal-doped oxides, electrochemical biosensors and other devices are employed in the detection of heavy metal ions, biosynthesised carbon dots are leading as a most capable material in the metal sensing applications. [16,35,36]. Table. 1 summarised the biomass-derived carbon dots from various sources and the application of Ferric ion detection with various limits of detection ranges.

In the present work, we demonstrate the synthesis of blue emission high fluorescence carbon dots from papaya seeds using the facile and eco-friendly microwave-assisted carbonisation process. The carbon dots synthesised by the one-pot efficient method doesn’t require any expensive chemicals, toxic reagents or complicated reaction conditions. Reaction time duration is minimized. The synthesised carbon dots are water-soluble and used as a fluorescence sensor probe for the detection of Fe3+ ions in an aqueous medium. The fluorescence detection process is a very simple and cost-effective method compared to other sophisticated methods.

Papaya seeds (PPY) were collected from the local market at Trichy, Tamil Nādu, India. The seeds were dried in sunlight to remove the moisture and ground into a fine powder which was stored in air tight sterile container. The analytical grade chemicals PbCl2, CaCl2, BaCl2, CuCl2, FeCl2, FeCl3, ZnCl2, NiCl2, MgCl2, CoCl2, CrCl2 and Sodium Hydroxide (NaOH) were obtained from SRL. 85% Ortho Phosphoric acid and Quinine sulphate (98%) from Merck and Ethanol was purchased from CSC. All chemicals were used as such without any purification. Double-deionized water has been used throughout experiments.

About 1 g of dried Papaya powder was taken in a 500 mL beaker and 10 mL of orthophosphoric acid (40%) was added with constant stirring of 5 to 10 mins. This reaction mixture was kept in a domestic microwave oven (LG, Model: MS2043DB) for 90 s until the carbonization of Papaya seeds completely took place. The beaker was cooled to room temperature and 30 mL of ethanol was added with continuous stirring. The blackish-brown residue obtained was filtered and the collected brown-coloured carbon dots solution was dried at room temperature.10 mL of distilled water was added to collect the water-soluble carbon dots which was then sonicated for 45 mins for dispersion. Then the dispersed carbon dots were filtered using 0.2 μm syringe filter and the supernatant liquid was collected and named as PPYCDs. Fig. 1 shows the schematic representation of the synthesis procedure of carbon dots from papaya seeds.

The fluorescence spectra were recorded in a Jasco FP-8300 spectrometer using a quartz cuvette with a slit range of 2.5 nm. UV–Visible absorption spectra were recorded by UV-1800 SHIMADZU and Fourier Transform Infra-Red Spectra (FTIR) by JASCO FT/IR-6600 with the wavelength range from 4000 to 400 cm−1. Particle morphology and size were measured using HRTEM (JEM-2100 Plus). The average size of the particles was measured using the ImageJ software tool and a photographic image was taken by Moto e40 mobile phone.

The photo stability of synthesized carbon dots was evaluated using photoluminescence spectra. Two different experiments were examined to evaluate the photostability of the synthesised PPYCDs. The synthesised carbon dots were kept at room temperature for 60 days and recorded fluorescence spectra at different intervals. Secondly, the carbon dots solution was irradiated with UV–Visible light at 365 nm wavelength for 60 mins and recorded the FL spectra.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of PPYCDs was calculated based on the literature, with quinine sulphate as a reference and using the following equation (1).
(1)
Q s
=
Q
r e f
(
I S
/
I
R e f
)
(
A
R e f
/
A S
)
(
η S
/
η
R e f
)

Where Qs is quantum yield of synthesised carbon dots. Qref quantum yield of standard (0.1 M H2SO4 as a solvent, Qs = 0.546 at 360 nm)), I is the integral areas of the emission intensity in the fluorescence spectrum, and A is the absorbance intensity and η solvent refractive index. The subscripts “S” and “ Ref “represent as a PPYCDs and standard quinine sulfate of synthesised carbon dots and standard. To avoid the reabsorption effects, the intensity of absorbance should be less than 0.1 for all samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Ferric ion in aqueous medium
ce:section.ce:para:
Freshly prepared metal chlorides such as PbCl2, CaCl2, BaCl2, CuCl2, FeCl2, FeCl3, ZnCl2, NiCl2, MgCl2, CoCl2, CrCl2 were dissolved in deionized water with 10 mmol concentration. 1 mL of PPYCDs and 1 mL of the metal solution was mixed in a 5 mL glass vial which was kept in UV light @365 nm. The same metal solutions were examined by fluorescence response with 325 nm excitation wavelength. Quenching behaviour of PPYCDs with various concentrations of Ferric ion (10 – 40 μmol) was observed. The static and dynamic quenching relationship was expressed by the Stern –Volmer Equation [37]
(2)
F 0
/ F = 1 + K s v
[ Q ]

Where F0 and F are fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of metal ion solution. Ksv- stern volume quenching constant, [Q] is metal ion concentration. The PPYCDs metal ion limit of detection (LOD) was estimated using the formula [20]
(3)
L O D =
3 σ
s

Where s is the slope of the calibration plot and
σ
is the standard deviation of the signals.

A simple microwave-assisted method has been adopted for the synthesis of carbon dots from biomass of papaya seeds associated with 40% concentration of ortho H3PO4. The synthesised carbon dots have good solubility, sensitivity and optical properties due to absorbance, excitation and emission behaviour. Figure S1 in supplementary data shows the graph of integrated area fluorescent intensity Vs. absorbance intensity for the reference (quinine sulfate) and PPYCDs. The slope value for Quinine sulfate is 5.7 × 105 and PPYCDs is 1.01X 105 which was calculated from the graphs. The quantum yield was about 9.7 % for PPYCDs. PPYCDs shows a bright blue colour at UV light@365 nm. The UV–visible absorption spectrum of PPYCDs has been shown in Fig. 2 .(a) where the two major absorption bands were noticed at 270 and 325 nm corresponding to π-π* transition of sp2 C = C bond and n-π* transition of the C = O bond, respectively. The inner pictures of Fig. 2.a represent the image of PPYCDs in the day and UV light. Fluorescence spectrum of Fig. 2(b) shows the emission and excitation peaks of the PPYCDs. The emission fluorescence spectrum of PPYCDs was shown in Fig. 3 (a) with various excitation wavelengths from 315 to 405 nm. While increasing the excitation wavelength, the fluorescence emission spectra gradually move towards redshift and emission peak intensity gradually decreases. The maximum emission intensity peak was observed at 335 nm excitation wavelength. Fig. 3(b) shows the different emission wavelengths with variation in the excitation wavelengths ranging from 315 nm to 405 nm which clearly explains that the emission peaks shift towards the red region or low energy region. The synthesised carbon dots show redshift, which was observed from fluorescence spectra. The photostability of the carbon dots was examined by irradiating UV–visible light for 60 min and recorded the fluorescence spectra which are shown in Fig. 4 a. The fluorescence intensity of the synthesised carbon dots clearly explains that carbon dots have good photostability in the presence of UV–visible light. PPYCDs did not observe any contamination in experimental duration because no significant change was observed in the intensity of the fluorescence spectra which was shown in Fig. 4b.

Fig. 5 . shows the FTIR spectra of dry papaya seeds powder (red colour) and synthesised PPY carbon dots. Various functional groups are present in the papaya seed powder where 2927 cm-1 and 2968 cm−1 represents saturated C–H bond and –CH3 sp3 carbon bonds. 1737 cm−1 stretching vibration is corresponded to carbonyl group (C = O) whereas 1216 cm−1 is corresponded to C-O stretching frequency. 899 cm−1 represents the phenyl C = C bond. PPYCDs (blue colour) FTIR spectra clearly denotes that papaya seeds have undergone the carbonization process. The peak observed at 3331 cm-1 broad in carbon dots represents the OH group and 1739 cm−1 peaks represent the carbonyl group (C = O) [16] and 1631 cm−1 represents the C = C bond. The peaks which appeared at 1213 cm−1 stretching is assigned for C-O bond and 891 cm−1 represents phenyl carbon bond. FTIR spectra clearly explains the carbonization process happened during microwave irradiation clearly denoting that synthesised carbon dots were decorated by carboxylic acid functional group.

The morphology of PPYCDs was examined using High-Resolution Transmission Electron Spectroscopy (HRTEM) which displayed the spherical shape of CDs well dispersed without aggregation. Fig. 6 . a. & d shows the HRTEM image of PPYCDs with a scale range 5 nm & 20 nm. The average size of the particles is about 1.45 nm, as shown in Fig. 6 b. The interplanar spacings (d) of PPYCDs is 0.21 nm which was measured from the HR-TEM image (Fig. 6. c).

Sensing perform was investigated using various metal ions solutions such as PbCl2, CaCl2, BaCl2, CuCl2, FeCl2, FeCl3, ZnCl2, NiCl2, MgCl2, CoCl2, CrCl2 with 10 mmol concentration. Aqueous PPYCDs added into metal solutions are kept in UV light @365 nm, where the blue colour luminescence disappeared in the mixture of PPYCDs Fe3+ solution Figure S2. This colour change represents the sensing of Fe3+ metal ions was confirmed by fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 7 a) shows the fluorescence spectra recorded for PPYCDs with various metal solutions at 335 nm excitation wavelength. Fe3+ ion metal solution emission peak intensity was suppressed due to the quenching of PPYCDs with the Fe3+ ions metal solution. The major two processes involved in quenching may be either the formation of a chelating complex with Fe3+ ions with a surface functional group of PPYCDs or by energy transfer from surface groups to the d-orbital of metal atoms. Owing to this complex formation, the electrons may be transferred from the excited state of PPYCDs to the d-orbital of Fe3+ ions and show a quenching phenomenon. These results are shown in the fluorescence response of a mixture of Fe3+ and PPYCDs solution. Other PPYCDs mixed metal solutions' fluorescence intensity peaks are near to PPYCDs intensity. Fig. 7. b shows the bar diagram of the F/F0 value of various metal ions with carbon dots. Fe3+ ions show a very low value which denotes that Fe3+ ions have a good quenching nature with newly synthesised carbon dots. From this confirmation, we have moved to evaluate the detection range of Fe3+ ions with various molar concentrations from 10 μM to 40 μM. PPYCDs are able to quench with Fe3+ ions with low concentration at room temperature. Fig. 8 a. shows the fluorescence spectra of various concentrations of Fe3+metal ion solution. The correlation coefficient R2 value is about 0.994 for the concentration range from 10 to 40 μM. Fig. 8b. shows the negative slope of the Fe3+ ion concentrations. The limit of detection (LOD) for PPYCDs is 2.35 μM and from the value it is clearly denoted that the quenching nature of the Fe3+ ions has strong absorption affinity with carbon dots due to high energy transfer between PPYCDs and ferric ions. The good selective and sensing properties helps to detect the ferric ion concentration in the polluted aqueous environment.

In summary, papaya seeds were used as a bio precursor to synthesise carbon dots by microwave-assisted carbonisation process in an eco-friendly approach. The average size of the PPYCDs is 1.45 nm and the quantum yield was about 9.7 %. The maximum excitation wavelength is 335 nm which gives strong fluorescence emission peaks at 405 nm. Synthesised carbon dots were decorated by carboxylic and hydroxyl groups which was confirmed by infrared spectra. The decorated functional groups participated in the quenching effect with Fe3+ ions in the aqueous medium. PPYCDs act as good sensor probes to selectively detect the Fe3+ ions with an LOD value of 2.35 μM. From these observations, we have concluded that the newly synthesised carbon dots have good fluorescence capacity, quantum yield and selectivity to sense the Fe3+ ions in an aqueous medium.

Hema Sindhuja George: Conceptualization, Resources, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Hosimin Selvaraj: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Andivelu Ilangovan: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Karthikeyan Chandrasekaran: Visualization, Validation. Velu Rajesh Kannan: Validation. Punniyakotti Parthipan: Formal analysis, Visualization. Bader O. Almutairi: Writing – review & editing. Ranjith Balu: Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Dr. Hosimin Selvaraj acknowledges UGC for the award of Dr. D. S. Kothari postdoctoral fellowship (DSKPDF) and School of Chemistry, Bharathidasan University. Dr. C. Karthikeyan acknowledges the Research Associate fellowship of RUSA 2.0 (Biological Science), Bharathidasan University, Trichy.
